coredata.dc:title: Facile and green synthesis of highly fluorescence carbon dots derived from Mucuna pruriens seeds: A versatile platform for sensitive detection of Au3+ ions and in vitro cell imaging

coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have established a simple, eﬃcient, non-toxic, cost-effective and completely green strategy to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) using Mucuna pruriens (alkushi) seeds. Synthesized CDs through facile one-pot hydrothermal route has a spherical shape with an average diameter of 4.02 nm. Surface analysis of these CDs was performed utilizing XPS, FTIR, and zeta potential measurement, confirming the presence of –OH, -NH2, and –COOH groups. The obtained CDs exhibit excellent photostability, high quantum yield and possess outstanding water solubility. Moreover, these CDs provide high response for detection of Au3+ ions through fluorescence quenching with a noticeable change in colour of the solution (colorimetric). The LOD was calculated to be 51.20 nM using 3σ/k rule. The mechanism of sensing was established through FTIR, florescence lifetime, UV and Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC). These experiments indicate that a ground state complex formed between CDs and Au3+ ions and subsequent reduction to Au0. The specificity towards Au3+ ions was tested against different interfering ions and was implemented to analysis the real sample. The synthesized CDs have also the potential to be applied in temperature sensing application. The cytotoxicity and biocompatibility were evaluated against prostate cancer cell line (PC3 cell) and a human lung fibroblast cell line (WI38 cell). Finally, Non-cytotoxic CDs were applied for live cell imaging and in vitro sensing of Au3+ ions.

. (A) XPS surface survey spectra of CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C1s, (C) N1s, (D) O1s.

(A) UV−vis absorption spectra of CDs (Wine line), FL emission spectra (λex = 330 nm) of the CDs (Blue line), inset: photographs of fluorescent CD solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (B) Excitation dependent FL emission spectra of CDs for the excitation from 300 to 390 nm.

. (A) Fluorescent emission spectra of CDs upon addition of various concentrations of Au3+ ions (λex = 330 nm). (B) The linear relationship between the F0/F value and the concentration of Au3+ ions within the range of 0−48 µM. (C) & (D) UV−vis absorption spectra of Au3+ ion in presence of varying concentrations of Au3+ ions.

Relative fluorescence emission intensity of CDs quenching by (A) Different ions, (B) Different amino acids at 330 nm excitation. Photographs of the CDs with various ions under (C) 365 nm UV light, (D) Day light.
Fluorescence decay curve of CDs (blue colour) and CDs in presence of Au3+ ions (green colour).

The top panel represents raw results for the sequential injection of Au3+ (100 µM) into CDs solution (200 nM). The bottom panel shows the integrated heat results after correction of heat of dilution against the mole ratio of Au3+/CDs. The points (closed circle) were fitted to a multiple-site model and the solid lines represent the best-fit results.

head.ce:title: Facile and green synthesis of highly fluorescence carbon dots derived from Mucuna pruriens seeds: A versatile platform for sensitive detection of Au3+ ions and in vitro cell imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we have established a simple, eﬃcient, non-toxic, cost-effective and completely green strategy to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) using Mucuna pruriens (alkushi) seeds. Synthesized CDs through facile one-pot hydrothermal route has a spherical shape with an average diameter of 4.02 nm. Surface analysis of these CDs was performed utilizing XPS, FTIR, and zeta potential measurement, confirming the presence of –OH, -NH2, and –COOH groups. The obtained CDs exhibit excellent photostability, high quantum yield and possess outstanding water solubility. Moreover, these CDs provide high response for detection of Au3+ ions through fluorescence quenching with a noticeable change in colour of the solution (colorimetric). The LOD was calculated to be 51.20 nM using 3σ/k rule. The mechanism of sensing was established through FTIR, florescence lifetime, UV and Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC). These experiments indicate that a ground state complex formed between CDs and Au3+ ions and subsequent reduction to Au0. The specificity towards Au3+ ions was tested against different interfering ions and was implemented to analysis the real sample. The synthesized CDs have also the potential to be applied in temperature sensing application. The cytotoxicity and biocompatibility were evaluated against prostate cancer cell line (PC3 cell) and a human lung fibroblast cell line (WI38 cell). Finally, Non-cytotoxic CDs were applied for live cell imaging and in vitro sensing of Au3+ ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent Carbon dot, a novel member of carbon nanomaterial family have fashioned enormous interest worldwide. This is primarily attributed due to their outstanding characteristics, such as remarkable stability, superior photoluminescence features, high water solubility, favourable biocompatibility, and excellent photo-electronic features [1–4]. These excellent characteristics of Carbon dots (CDs) distinguish them from the conventional luminescent substances and being extensively applied in numerous fields such as bio imaging [5,6], drug delivery [7,8] and cancer therapy in biomedical fields [7,9], photo catalysis [10,11], light emitting diodes [12,13], solar cells [14,15], fingerprint detection [16,17] and so forth [2,18]. However they are also applied for different fluorescent sensing purposes like detection of cations and anions [19–21], chemical Warfare Agents [22], Organic fungicide [23], and the molecules like amino acids [24], nitro aromatic compounds [25], hydrogen peroxide [26] etc. Since the ﬁrst accidental discovery in 2004 [27], numerous synthetic approaches, such as microwave method [28], hydrothermal method [29], chemical ablation method [30], ultrasonic method [31], solvothermal method [32], laser ablation method [33], and electrochemical method [34] have been employed to prepare CDs. The precursors of different types have also been utilized to prepare various types of CDs [35,36]. However, majority of these conventional methods have several limitations, like severe synthesis conditions, low quantum yields, laborious and complex processes, use of environmentally and biologically hazardous agents etc. Among the other techniques, hydrothermal approach is the simplistic technique for preparation of CDs.

Numerous green approaches have been established to synthesize ﬂuorescent CDs by using renewable inexpensive natural precursors such as Prawn-Shell [37], coffee grounds [38], tobacco [29], coconut husk [39], honey [40], carrot [41] unripe peach [42], garlic [43], glucose [44], curcumin [45], durian Peel [46] chitosan [47], algal blooms [48], orange peel [49], crab shell [50], spider silk [51] etc. In most of the cases, surface passive agents were added to stabilize and enhance the fluorescent character of the CDs. However, we have utilized no such chemical and surface passive agents to prepare CDs.

In our study, we have developed new CDs using Mucuna pruriens seeds (alkushi seeds), which is a beans that grows in several tropical residences including Africa, South America and Asia. It has L-DOPA content and has been known to show anti-parkinson and neuroprotective effects [52]. The inherent properties of the source material might be add an interesting dimension and potentially introducing unique qualities to these synthesized CDs.

Gold has been widely utilized in various industrious application such as electrical devices like mobile and computer, space exploration, pollution control etc [53,54]. However, gold complexes have demonstrated their efficacy in combating rheumatoid arthritis and tuberculosis. Au (III) complexes are the potential candidate for use as an anticancer drug because of its tumor growth inhibiting properties. However, gold nano particles are successfully employed in biomedical field like drug delivery, cancer treatment [55,56].

Though metallic gold (Au0) is less toxic to people’s health, Au3+ ions and its compounds are regarded to be potentially toxic to human body. Au3+ can bind strongly to single deoxynucleotide and various enzymes that leads to severe damage to kidneys, liver and peripheral nervous system [57]. It has been observed that HAuCl4 at the concentration of 10–15 μmol can cause agglutination and hemolysis of erythrocytes (Red Blood Corpuscles) [58]. The poisonous effect of Au3+ to cause nephrotoxicity, bone marrow depression, blood dyscriasis etc. have been informed [59]. Consequently, detection of Au3+ ions in all living systems and environment is very important.

Conventional analytical techniques, such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS) [60], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [61] can be employed for detection Au3+ ions. But, these instruments are very costly and require time-consuming sample preparation, sophisticated instruments and highly skilled individuals. In contrast, the fluorescence sensing procedure comprises highly sensitive, easy operation, more robust and simplistic approach to detect metal ions. Numerous fluorescent probes for detection of Au3+ ions have been informed such as, Sing-Ming Chan and his team have reported fluorescent metal ion sensors using [2] Rotaxanes for detection of Au3+ ions [62]. Ji Eun Park and co-workers investigated the chemosignaling of Au3+ by using thiocoumarin as precursor [63]. But these involve the need of complex synthetic procedure with comparatively high detection limit.

The literatures offer a limited number CDs as fluorescence probes for the detection of Au3+ ions. Jie Liao and his team were developed nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for fluorimetric detection of Au3+ ions using a mixture of peach gum polysaccharide and ethylenediamine, as a surface passivating agent [64]. In similar fashion, an N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) has been developed by Zeinab Rahmani and his co-worker using gum tragacanth and ethylenediamine to detect Au3+ ion [65]. However, the potential for a green synthetic strategy lies in the eco-friendly synthesis of CDs using non-toxic originators sourced directly from nature, environmentally friendly solvents, and renewable raw materials. Nevertheless, we have not come across any existing reports so far on the CDs as fluorescence probe synthesized directly from natural source without the presence of surface passive agents for the purpose of detecting Au3+ ions.

Here, we have introduced a straightforward, one-pot hydrothermal method for synthesizing non-cytotoxic and highly fluorescent CDs using Alkushi (Mucuna pruriens) seeds as precursor, without any surface modification or the involvement of additional passive surface agents. With an average size of 4.02 nm, the synthesized CDs exhibited hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups on their surface. However, our synthesized CDs demonstrated exceptional luminescent properties, displaying robust photostability and maintaining stable fluorescence characteristics even when exposed to alterations in ionic strength. Moreover, the CDs exhibits a sensitive and selective sensing response to detect the Au3+ ion and the efficacy of detection was studied in various real water samples. More significantly, the CDs exhibit visible colour change (pink) through naked eye by introducing Au3+ ions selectively. Thus, our synthesized CDs can function as an effective probe for fluorometric as well as colorimetric detection of Au3+ ions. Additionally, our produced CDs have the capability to be utilized in temperature sensing applications. The mechanism of sensing was established through different spectroscopic and Isothermal titration calorimetric (ITC) method. The isothermal calorimetric method indicates the interaction was highly exothermic in nature and a negative entropy driven, confirms the formation of coagulated complex which then subsequently forms gold nano particle. Additionally, the obtained CDs exhibited no cytotoxic effects, as evidenced by the MTT assay conducted on PC3 and WI38 cell. Furthermore, they can be successfully used for bioimaging and in-vitro detection of Au3+ ions, as confirmed by the imaging of PC3 cells.

The utilization of renewable and green natural sources for its economical production, straightforward synthesis process, greater photo stability, improved solubility in water, all contribute to the rationalization of this material (CDs) as an environmentally friendly choice.

The efficiency of CDs is based on their ability to sense temperature, ability to detect toxic Au3+ ions selectively, non-cytotoxicity, and their potential for bioimaging and in-vitro detection of Au3+ ions.

Alkushi seeds were collected from the forest near to Vidyasagar University, cleaned by using distilled water and then dried. To prepare phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solutions of various pH values, Phosphate-buffered saline was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany. The salts NaNO3, Mn(NO3)2, Ba(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2.6 H2O, Ni(NO3)2.6 H2O, Co(NO3)2.6 H2O, Al(NO3)3.9 H2O, AgNO3, HAuCl4.3 H2O, PtCl2, Cd(NO3)2.4 H2O, Sr(NO3)2, MgNO3.6 H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3.9 H2O, Ca(NO3)2.4 H2O, FeSO4.7 H2O, CrCl3.6 H2O, Na3AsO4.7 H2O, used for the sensing purpose were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation. All chemicals used to carry out the experiment were of analytical reagent grade and were employed as such without additional puriﬁcation. The solutions required for entire study were prepared by using de-ionized water.

UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded by using Shimadzu UV1800 Spectro-photometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared Spectra) experiments were performed on a Perkin Elmer spectrum (Perkin-Elmer, USA) in the range of 500–4000 cm−1. The scan-rate was 2 cm−1/s during the FTIR measurements. All the fluorescence spectra were carried out on Perkin Elmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer (USA) with a 1.0 cm quartz cell fitted out with thermostatic bath. The width of the excitation and emission slit were fixed at 5 and 10 nm respectively during the course of study. The room is maintained throughout the study and emission wavelength was recorded from 350 nm to 550 nm ranges.

HRTEM (High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy) images of the CDs were obtained using a JEOL-2100 HRTEM operated with the acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Samples for HRTEM observation were equipped by putting a drop of Carbon dots (CDs) solution on an amorphous carbon-coated copper grid and then dried in vacuum.

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) measurement were conducted using Park XE7 (Park Systems, South Korea). The samples were prepared by depositing few drops of sample solutions on a silicon glass surface, subsequently drying the solution under vacuum.

The zeta potential of CDs solution was determined by using Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (UK) using a laser beam of 532 nm through laser Doppler electrophoresis process at 25 °C. Disposable folded capillary cell of 1-cm optical path length was employed to determine the zeta potential of CDs at a concentration of 0.01 mg/mL. The measurement quality factor zeta-potential measurement was good.

X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained using an a PHI 5000 Versa probe II scanning XPS microprobe (ULVAC-PHI, U.S.) equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα excitation source (1486.6 eV).

The fluorescence lifetime of the CDs was determined with a Delta Flex-Moduler Fluorescence Life time system, Horiba Scientific, (UK). Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) method was employed with excitation and emission wavelength at 288 nm and 402 nm respectively.

Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC) experiments were carried out using high subtle Nano ITC Standard Volume from TA instruments (Waters, New Castle, USA) with the calorimeter cell of volume 0.95 mL. 10 µL Au3+ solution (100 µM) were successively injected from a 250 μL rotating syringe (290 rpm) into the isothermal sample chamber containing 200 nM of CDs solution at the temperature 25 °C. The corrected heat of the interaction obtained from the experiments by subtracting the heat of dilution experiments performed under identical experimental conditions. The solutions were degassed under vacuum unit supplied by TA instruments to eliminate air bubbles before ITC experiment. The results obtained from the experiment were analysed using NanoAnalyse software to estimate the binding stoichiometry (N), binding affinity (K), binding entropy (ΔS), binding free energy (ΔG) and the enthalpy of binding (ΔH).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs from the alkushi (Mucuna pruriens) seeds
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent CDs were synthesized from alkushi (Mucuna pruriens) seeds by hydrothermal approach. Alkushi seeds were washed with distilled water and alcohol several time to remove impurities. Then washed seeds were dried under sunlight and powdered. 2 g of powder was mixed with 40 mL of ultrapure water and allowed to react hydrothermally in a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave for 6 h at 150 °C. The heated mixture was cooled to room temperature and product was centrifuged for 12 min at 15,000 rpm to eradicate the unreacted and large particles. Subsequently, the resulting yellow solution was subjected to filtration using a 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h. Finally the synthesized CDs were preserved at 4 °C for characterisation and for further experiments.

The fluorescence QY of synthesized CDs was measured by using Rhodamine 6 G as the reference standard (quantum yield 95 % at 488 nm in EtOH). The relative fluorescence QY of the synthesized CDs were calculated using the following equation [66]

where, the subscripts “CD” and “ST” denote the synthesized CDs (tested sample) and standard sample, “Q” represents quantum yield, “m” represents the slope of the integrated FL emission intensity plotted against absorbance values at various concentrations, and “η” is the refractive index of the solvent. The determined quantum yield was 20.40 %. Additionally, we have determined the quantum yield of CDs when exposed to different ions. It was observed that the quantum yield of CDs significantly diminishes with Au3+ ions, while the influence of other metal ions on the quantum yield remains relatively minor. The detailed quantum yield of the CDs in presence of different ions are summarised in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Au3+ ions by the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solution for the experiment was prepared by adding 0.01 mg/mL of CDs in 10 mM PBS buffer solution of pH 7. In this titration procedure, an increased concentrations of Au3+ ions (0–48 μM) were added to 1 mL stock solution of CDs in a quartz cuvette and equilibrated for 10 min prior to obtaining the FL spectrum. Fluorescence emission measurements were executed using excitation wavelength 330 nm at 25 °C temperature. The UV−vis experiments were carried out in presence and absence of Au3+ ions, using same stock solutions within the wavelength range of 225 nm to 700 nm.

To compare the selectivity of CDs towards Au3+ ions, the FL intensity was tested against different monovalent, bivalent and trivalent ions such as Mg2+, Na+, Cr3+, Pt2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Sr2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Al3+, Pb2+ and AsO4 3- (48 μM). Each experiment was executed under same experimental condition as stated above.

Temperature sensing experiments were performed by heating and cooling CDs in Fluorescence Peltier system (PTR-1) at different temperatures. The investigation temperature was between 15 °C and 75 °C with the interval of 5 °C. The corresponding FL intensity of the CDs was obtained by using excitation at 330 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Human cell lines & cell culture conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The PC3 and WI38 cell lines were derived from a prostate cancer patient and a human lung fibroblast cell line, respectively. The PC3 and WI38 cell lines were cultivated in RPMI 1640 and MEM mediums, supplemented with 10 % FBS. Penicillin and streptomycin (100 units /mL) were added into the respective medium. The cells were subjected to incubation at 37 °C in an environment containing 5 %CO2 and 95 % relative humidity (RH).

The required cell lines were seeded in 48 well plates and subjected to incubation at 37 °C (5 % CO2). After 18–20 h incubation, several concentrations of CDs were used to treat the cells and incubated for 18–20 h. The next day the media was discard, fresh MTT ((3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) solution (5 mg/mL) was added and re-incubated for 3 h at 37 °C. Finally, the MTT solution was discard and then extraction buffer was added to form a formazan crystal violet colour. After that absorbance spectra was measured at 570 nm by using UV-Vis spectroscopy [67].

The PC3 cell lines were seeded in an 18 mm coverslip and incubated at 37 °C (5 %CO2) for 18–20 h. After that, the cells were treated with CDs and various concentrations of Au3+ ions. Then it was incubated for 2 h. Then the cells were mounted with 50 % glycerol and observed fluorescent intensity was recorded under a fluorescent microscope (Leica, Wetzlar, Germany) [68].

TEM and AFM were utilized to investigate the structure, morphology and distribution of the newly synthesized CDs. TEM image illustrates that the prepared CDs remain regularly dispersed without any aggregation. They displayed a spherical shape, with a size distribution spanning from 2 to 6.5 nm ( Fig. 1 A & B). From the size distribution histogram of 39 particles, it was revealed that the average diameter of the particle of CDs was found to be 4.02 nm.

The two-dimensional AFM image demonstrate that CDs being well-dispersed and having a quasi-spherical morphology. Based on the AFM height images (Fig. 1C & D), it is evident that the CDs exhibits an average height of 3–5 nm. The result aligns with the findings obtained from the TEM characterization.

FTIR spectrum of CDs was recorded to identify the nature of functional group existing on their surface. The FTIR spectrum of CDs is shown in the Fig. 2. In this spectrum, the peak at 3548 cm−1and 3280 cm−1 can be assigned to the corresponding stretching vibration of O-H and N-H groups respectively [50] which enhances the stability and hydrophilic character of CDs in aqueous medium. The existence of amino groups is further reinforced by the C-N stretching vibration observed at 1152 cm−1 [59]. The peak at 3077.5 cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibrations of =C-H bond and the signal at 2986 cm−1 corresponds to the sp3 C-H stretching vibration of C-H bond [48]. The peak at 1640 cm–1 and 1769 cm–1 conclude that the surface of the CDs is composed of carboxylic acids [65]. A strong absorption band at 1558 cm−1 is due to stretching vibration of CC moiety of aromatic and/or conjugated structure [65]. The peak at 1465 cm−1 is assigned to in-plane bending vibrations of the methylene group [48,50] and 1394 cm–1 can be ascribe to the symmetrical deformation of –CH3 [50]. The weak absorption peaks at 1258 cm−1 and 1025 cm−1 are coincided with the C-O stretching vibrations of carboxylic acid and C-O-C bond respectively [19]. The results showing that hydrophilic functional groups such as hydroxyl groups, amino groups and carboxyl groups were present on the surface of synthesized CDs.

Furthermore, the XPS was performed to confirm the composition of the surface of CDs. The major elemental constituents of CDs are 60.72 atom% carbon, 38.07 atom% oxygen, and 1.21 atom% nitrogen. The full scan XPS spectrum ( Fig. 3A) of the CDs exhibits three peaks at 285.2 eV, 399.6 eV and 531.6 eV, corresponded to C1s, N1s and O1s respectively [19].

The high resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3B) comprises four peaks at 284.68 eV, 285.92 eV, 286.57 eV and 287.75 eV, which should attributed to the C-C, C–N, C–OH, and CO bonds respectively [37]. The high resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) involves of two peaks at 399.63 eV and 401.97 eV, which should be revealed to the C–N and N–H bonds respectively [19]. The peaks at 531.66 eV and 533.86 eV of the O1s spectrum (Fig. 3D) should assigned to the CO, C–OH bonds respectively [37]. These results comply with the results obtained from FT-IR spectroscopy that the surface of synthesized CDs contains diverse oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups.

Further, our synthesized CDs has zeta potential (ζ) value −12.1 mV (Figure S7). Negative zeta potential provides additional evidence and further confirms the abundance of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the periphery of the CDs. These functional groups play a crucial role in promoting a favourable dispersion of CDs in water by engaging in hydrogen bonding with water molecules.

The dilute CDs solution is pale yellow in daylight and radiates bright blue fluorescence when exposed to a UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm. UV−vis absorption spectrum was recorded at room temperature. Fig. 4A shows two characteristics

peaks, one strong absorption at 265 nm and a weak peak at 332 nm in UV−vis spectrum which are attributed due to π-π* transition for CC bonds in the core and n-π* transition for CO bond at the periphery respectively [37]. Our synthesized CDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence characteristics like the majority of luminous carbon nanoparticles [19,59,64], and it is represented in the Fig. 4B. The excitation ranging from 300 to 390 nm with the wavelength interval of 10 nm shows that maximum emission peak at 402 nm was obtained at the excitation of 330 nm. Hence, we used the excitation 330 nm for all the measurement of fluorescence spectra.

Fluorescence emission behaviour of the CDs was studied in various conditions such as time, irradiation times, ionic and strength pH values. Our synthesized CDs demonstrate a consistent and reliable fluorescence characteristic ( Fig. 5A). The CDs exhibited no sign of aggregation and retained their homogeneity when dissolved in de-ionised water during the monitoring period of 30 days. Furthermore, the FL intensity remains almost identical when it was preserved at 4 °C temperature at this period (Figure S8). To evaluate resistance of CDs toward photolysis, CDs were irradiated with UV light at 365 nm for a duration of 1.5 h and fluorescence emission were recorded after a fixed interval. The Fig. 5B indicates that fluorescence properties of the CDs remained almost intact with irradiation time.

To appraise the stability of CDs under high ionic environments, FL intensities in presence of various concentrations of sodium chloride were recorded. The Fig. 5C illustrates a minor fluctuation in FL intensity within the NaCl concentration range of 0 M to 150 mM. The capacity to withstand with varying saline concentrations plays an important role in determining its suitability as a probe in bio-imaging and can be appropriate as fluorescence probe even in marine water.

The luminescent properties of the CDs was characterized at different pH conditions and represented in Fig. 5D. The FL intensity of CDs enhances with increasing pH from 2.5 to 7 but decreases from 7 to 9.5, although the change of FL intensity is very small. This phenomenon can be explained by the protonation or deprotonation of the functional groups situated on the surface of the CDs when exposed to acidic or alkaline media.

To establish the suitability of CDs for thermometry applications, the temperature-dependent fluorescence was investigated. The Fig. 6A represents temperature dependent fluorescence spectra of CDs upon excitation at 330 nm, demonstrating that the FL intensity of the CDs decreases with increasing the temperature over the range of 15–75 °C (Fig. 6A) without any shifting of emission peak (Fig. 6D). Fig. 6B indicates the integrated fluorescence at various temperature. The integrated fluorescence intensity exhibited a good linear response over the whole investigated temperature range with a correlation coefficient R2= 0.99604 The reversibility of the temperature-dependent fluorescence of CDs were also estimated within the temperature range and the result shows their good capability of recovering FL-intensity to initial state (Fig. 6C). The consequence ensure that temperature does not cause any irreversible damage to CDs.

The change of FL intensity over increase in temperature can be explicated by the fact that radiative surface population enhanced at low temperature whereas non-radiative surface population recombination dominates at high temperature. As a result, more electrons in an excited state were able to revert back to ground state through a non-radiative process. Consequently, this temperature-sensitive fluorescent characteristics of these CDs can be efficiently employed for various thermometry applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing response of CDs towards Au3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Interesting, fluorescence behaviour of CDs incited us to explore the detection of Au3+ ions by the fluorescence quenching method. On addition of Au3+ ions, a gradual quenching of FL intensity at 402 nm was clearly detected ( Fig. 7A), which postulates that the FL intensity of CD solution is sensitive towards Au3+ ions. The original FL intensity of the CDs was reduced by 76 % after gradual addition of 48 μM of Au3+ ions.

Absorption spectra of CDs solution was recorded after gradual addition of Au3+ ions, spanning from 0 to 48 μM range as represented in Fig. 7C. The absorbance of CDs solution increases and, a strong and wide absorption band appeared at 545 nm after gradual addition of Au3+ ions (Fig. 7D). This result indicates the complexation between CDs and Au3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimum condition for detection of Au3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: To attain the optimum analytical conditions for detection of Au3+ ions, numerous factors (like reaction time, solution pH, salt concentration) affecting the FL intensity were optimized. After 8 minutes of adding Au3+ ions at a definite concentration (6.0 μM), a stable fluorescence intensity was achieved (Figure S9). This implies that interaction between CDs and Au3+ ions achieves an equilibrium state at that time interval (Figure S10). To acquire better precision, fluorescence intensities of each sample was recorded after 10 min of quenching. The salt dependent fluorescence responses of CDs indicate that salt concentration does not alter the FL intensity of CDs in the range between 0 and 150 mM. The pH dependent FL intensity indicates the maximum at the pH 7 (Fig. 5D). Hence, in the present work, we used 10 mM PBS buffer solution of pH 7.0 to acquire greater accuracy at room temperature (25°C).

We recorded fluorescence response of CDs at 402 nm in presence of Au3+ ions using excitation light of 330 nm. The emission spectra of CDs, as depicted in the Fig. 7A, demonstrates a decline in intensity with increasing concentration of Au3+ ions (ranging from 0 to 48 μM). The quenching efficiency (F0/F) exhibits an excellent linearity (R2= 0.99967) across the concentration range of Au3+ ions from 0 to 1.75 μM (Fig. 7B). The limit of detections (LOD) of Au3+ was calculated to be 51.20 nM using the IUPAC conventional method LOD = 3σ/k. The obtained LOD value reveals that present sensor has a superior lowest detecting limit which is better than majority of the previously reported outcomes ( Table 1).

Considering the intricacy of environment, the effectiveness of fluorescent probes is contingent upon their selectivity, making it an imperative parameter to consider. The effect on the FL intensity of CDs in presence of several environmentally significant ions, including Na+, Ba2+, Sr2+, Cr3+, Pt2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Co2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Al3+ and AsO4 3- were tested. Among all the tested ions, only Au3+ ions exhibited the ability to quench FL intensity of the CDs effectively, while other ions displayed a slight or negligible fluorescence quenching effect ( Fig. 8A & 8 C). The obtained outcomes indicate that our prepared CDs have excellent specific selectivity toward Au3+ ions.

Again, UV–vis spectra of CDs with other competitive ions did not exhibit any significant absorption band at 545 nm (Figure S11). Simultaneously, the CDs solution turns pink upon the addition of Au3+ ions, visible to the naked eye (Fig. 8D). This phenomenon also revealed the specificity of CDs towards Au3+ ions as we obtained from the FL quenching study.

Again, we also investigated the interference experiment in presence of others ions under same conditions, ensuring a concentration ratio of 1:1 (Au3+ ion: Other ions). The results are presented in figure S12 which suggests that other ions influence only a slight on the fluorescence detection of Au3+ ions. We also conducted fluorescent study of CDs in the presence of mixed metal ions, both with and without Au3+ ions in same ratio concentration. Figure S13 revealed that the FL intensities of CDs were significantly quenched only when Au3+ ions were present.

Additionally, we have investigated the sensitivity of CDs towards diverse amino acids, namely Gly, Val, Ile, Trp, Met, Leu, Tyr, Ala, His, Asp and Arg. Fig. 8B illustrates that the investigational amino acids exhibit negligible interferences ability. Hence from the obtained result, we can culminate that our synthesized CDs have the capability to be utilised for sensitive and selective detection of Au3+ ion in live cells.

The most plausible reasons for the quenching of FL intensity are fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), lowering of pH upon the addition of Au3+, reductive electron transfer from CDs to Au3+, dynamic collision quenching at excited state, coordination-induced aggregation or ground state complex formation etc.

Au3+ ion exhibits no absorption peak above 300 nm in aqueous or PBS buffer solution. Absence of spectral overlap between emission spectrum of our prepared CDs and absorption spectrum of Au3+ reject the possibilities of fluorescence resonance energy transfer from CDs to Au3+ [19]
. That finding is reinforced by unaltered lifetime curve observed before and after reaction between CDs and Au3+ ions ( Fig. 9). In contrast to majority of previously reported CDs [19,39], change of luminescent intensity of our CDs is very slight with variation of pH. This rejects the idea of strong reduction of FL intensity of CDs on addition of very small change of acidity (Au3+) to the system.

To acquire more comprehensive knowledge about quenching phenomena, we executed a Stern−Volmer analysis using the equation as follows
F

K

Where F0 and F are fluorescence (FL) intensity of synthesized CDs in absence and presence of Au3+ ion respectively. Ks is the Stern−Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of quencher (Au3+). The plot of F0/F against concentration of Au3+ shows a significant linear correlation (correlation coefficient, R2= 0.99967) as depicted in the Fig. 7B.

To realize the fluorescence decay kinetics of CDs, TCSPC (time-correlated single photon counting) experiments were conducted in presence and absence of Au3+ ions at excitation wavelength of 288 nm. The curve was fitted using Tri-exponential fit decay by the following expression.

Where F(t) is the normalized fluorescence decay, τ1, τ2 and τ3 are decay times, A1, A2 and A3 are the corresponding amplitudes respectively.

The average life time was estimated using the given equation.

Table 2 illustrates that the average life time of CDs is 1.90 ns. After addition of Au3+ ions, the average fluorescence lifetime transformed to 1.83 ns i.e. lifetime of the prepared CDs did not display any substantial alteration. This spectacle reject the concept of dynamic collision quenching at excited state and non-radiative transfer of electrons from the excited state of CDs to the Au3+. Consequently, the observed quenching can be explained by the static mechanism.

Absorbance of our synthesized CDs increases after gradual addition of Au3+ ions and interestingly a wide band appear at 545 mn (Fig. 7D). Vadivel Ramanan and his co-workers [59] proposed coordination-induced aggregation for such fluorescence quenching effect but didn't observe any surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak of AuNPs at 545 mn. Hence they ruled out the formation of Au3+ nano particle. But in this experiment, we observed initial straw yellow coloured solution changes to pink when allowed to stand for 1 h after addition of Au3+ ions which suggests the potential formation of Au nanoparticles (Fig. 8D) [69,70].

As shown in Fig. 2, the intensity of the characteristic bands of -OH at 3548 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of CDs is decreased considerably and position of the peak shifted to 3453 cm−1 after accumulation of Au3+ ions, whereas intensity of N-H stretching vibration (3280 cm–1) is reduced significantly and C-N (1152 cm–1) stretching peak of amines shifted to 1155 cm–1. Again, stretching vibration peak of carboxylic acid (1640 cm–1) was shifted to 1630 cm–1.This results established that the interaction with the metal ions was mostly led by the –OH and –NH2 and -COOH groups and based on the previous research, these group have the potential to serve as reducing agents. These functional groups react with Au3+ ions to form coagulated complex which permits transport of electrons from C-dots to Au3+ ions, that ultimately leads to the creation of gold nanoparticles [71,72]. Consequently, resulting Au nanoparticle causes fluorescence quenching and transform into pink colour which can be visible easily in daylight ( Scheme 1).

The thermodynamics of the interaction between CDs and Au3+ ions were investigated by highly sensitive isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC). ITC analysis deliver detailed information on thermodynamic profile like enthalpy change (ΔH), entropy change (TΔS), free energy change (ΔG) and also together with stoichiometry (n) and binding constant (K). The calorimetric ITC profile of the titration of Au3+ to CDs is depicted in Fig. 10.

The upper panel signifies characteristic raw data for successive injection of Au3+ to CDs solution and the lower panel displays the integrated heat as a function of mole ratio after subtracting the corresponding heat of dilution. The data points represent the continuous line which signifies the calculated best fit to the data. The results of the ITC experiment are summarised in the Table 3. The binding constant of CDs to Au3+ was estimated to be 1.26×106 M−1. The interaction between CDs and Au3+ ions was driven by the stronger negative enthalpy and negative entropy value. The negative value of the entropy clearly indicates an agglomeration occurs during the interaction between CDs and Au3+ ions by the formation of coordination complex.
The ITC data provides additional evidence, reinforcing the idea of complex formation between CDs and Au3+ ions that results in FL quenching.

To estimate the practical applications of our synthesized CDs as sensors, they were applied directly to detect Au3+ ions in real water samples. The process involves standard addition of Au3+ ions with different concentration in real samples collected from the Kangsabati river, tap water from the university campus and pond water from nearby locality. Each of the collected sample was centrifuged at 9000 rpm and then filtered using 0.22 μm membrane before investigation. Then determination of recovery was done based on fluorescence. The findings obtained were documented in Table 4 . The findings suggest that recoveries lie in the range of 96.66–103.30 % and the RSDs were less than 2.564 %. Hence the proposed method displayed great accuracy, reliable and repeatable towards the real sample analysis.

The MTT assay is a valuable tool for the quantification of cell viability, proliferation, and cytotoxicity study. The MTT assay suggested that the CDs treated PC3 and WI38 cell lines were 100 % viable compared to the untreated cell (Figure S14 & S15). Therefore, our synthesized CDs is a biocompatible material and appropriate for biomedical applications.

In this experiment, we have used prostate cancer cell line PC3, and the outcomes are depicted in Fig. 11. The presence of free CDs the cells shows a very bright blue emission. When Au3+ ions of 15 μM, 30 μM and 48 μM were added, the fluorescent intensity cells gradually decreased due the interaction between Au3+ ions and CDs. Therefore, this result suggest that these CDs can be utilized proliﬁcally for blue fluorescent cells imaging and presence of Au3+ ions in the live cells.

Briefly, we have established one-pot hydrothermal procedure to produce fluorescent CDs using Mucuna pruriens seeds as natural precursor. The produced CDs exhibit a high quantum yield (QY) of 20.40 %, consistent particle size (average diameter of 4.02 nm), and possess hydrophilic surface functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl. These CDs exhibits excellent water solubility, brilliant optical properties, and high photo stability even in presence of high salt concentrations.

CDs significantly shows fluorometric and colorimetric response towards Au3+ ions. The limit of detection (LOD) for Au3+ ions was calculated to be 51.20 nM. The sensor exhibits effectiveness in analysis of real samples based on fluorescence. Additionally, the non-toxic and highly biocompatible nature of CDs makes them appropriate for bioimaging probes, with the added capability of sensing Au3+ ions in live cells. Therefore, the advantages of this work are:

1. Convenient, eco-friendly, green, simplistic and low-cost hydrothermal approach to synthesis CDs without any use of chemicals or surface passive agent 2. Synthesized CDs has high quantum yield and good photo stability towards high ionic environment. 3. It can be effectively employed to detect Au3+ ions through fluorometric as well as colorimetric response. 4. The non-cytotoxic CDs shows good biocompatibility for imaging and proficient to sense Au3+ in live cells. Thus we contemplate that experimental findings from this investigation will provide valuable insights to design metal-ion sensors through eco-friendly and green route which may applicable in various chemical and biological field.
